body_txt: Some people believe that Ambition Is Evil , usually because people often do terrible things to others in the name of attaining said ambitions. Some works even go so far as to portray unambitious people as good because of their lack of ambition. On the other hand, some works go in the other direction, portraying a lack of ambition or drive as a bad thing. This view might be expressed by the narrative or by individual characters. The idea that a lack of ambition is bad has many rationalizations. Some might argue that society advances because of ambitious people, and those without ambition simply do their job rather than advance society. Another belief is that those without ambition are lazy, as they only do the bare minimum of what's required of them rather than striving for excellence. Others might claim that the unambitious are cowardly as they prefer to avoid risks and stick to what's comfortable rather than face the possibility of failure. Finally, there is also the criticism that those without ambition are stagnant and refuse to grow and change as people. Characters who hold this view tend to naturally be those who hold grand ambitions themselves. These types of characters tend to look down on those who have no ambition or whose ambitions are too small for their standards. In stories with villains who believe that Ambition Is Evil , the heroes will often refute that view by pointing out the positive aspects of ambition. Stories that have heroes who embody said positive aspects will sometimes be contrasted by villains who embody the negative aspects of a lack of ambition. In stories where the heroes grew up struggling, their ambition will often be born out of a desire for a better life, while villains will often be spoiled people who have no ambition because they've had everything handed to them. Just as the idea that ambition is inherently evil is sometimes challenged by some stories, other stories might challenge the idea that a lack of ambition is inherently a bad thing. Some stories might have a character who's truly content with humble circumstances be criticized for their lack of desire to improve their lot in life, with the story emphasizing that personal happiness matters more than societal definitions of success. If a character has what others would consider a "lowly" ambition, the story might promote the idea that hopes and dreams can take on many forms, and even "petty" dreams can be just as fulfilling as more grandiose ones. Some stories might even balance this trope with Ambition Is Evil by stating that while being overly ambitious is bad, having little or no ambition at all isn't exactly a good thing. See also The Creon , who is often portrayed as unconventional for not wanting to lead. Compare Living Is More than Surviving , in which living a fulfilling life is viewed as more positive than simply being alive, and Enemies Equals Greatness , where having no or few enemies is viewed as a sign that someone is a failure in life. Compare/contrast Ambitious, but Lazy , where being ambitious without a work ethic is portrayed as bad. A Coming of Age Story might have this narrative, with the hero starting off as a Lazy Bum and/or The Slacker , but developing into a more ambitious person later on. Someone who has Happiness in Minimum Wage might be viewed as this.
